en.extract:
The small tree finch (Camarhynchus parvulus) is a bird species belonging to the Darwin's finch group within the tanager family Thraupidae.
It has a grasping beak with curved culmens. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forests and subtropical or tropical dry shrubland. During the non-breeding season it is known to form large groups with small ground-finches.
It is an endemic species to the Galapagos islands and its conservation status has been listed as "Least Concern". However, bird counts which have been conducted since 1997 have shown a continuous decline in small tree-finch populations in the Scalesia and agricultural zones of Santa Cruz, where the small tree-finch is most abundant. Small tree-finch counts have remained stable in the transition zone (also quite abundant here) and in the less popular dry and fern zones on Santa Cruz island. This species is in particularly impacted by the larvae of the parasitic avian vampire fly (Philornis downsi).


Distribution

The small tree finch is found on all the main islands except Española, Genovesa, Marchena, Darwin, and Wolf. It was also absent in the most recent census (2017–2018) on Rábida, Pinta, and Santa Fé islands.


Mating
Darwin's tree finches build display domed nests and use these to attract mates and for nesting. Male small tree finches will sing close to their nest once it has been built, to attract female small tree finches. When these arrive, they inspect the display nest by entering it. Thereafter they will either choose to accept both the male and the display nest, accept the male but reject the nest (so they will then build a new nest together), or reject both the male and the display nest.
Older males build more concealed nests, typically higher within the tree canopy, compared to younger males, which reduces predation on the nests. Females preferentially choose to breed with older males with whom they have higher fledging success compared to younger males. In a study by Kleindorfer (2007), predation accounted for 60% of nest loss, making it a strong selective force. Males with high vocal performance, which is predicted by beak morphology, also have higher pairing success. There is also evidence for size-assortative pairing based on beak and tarsus length. Thus multiple factors influence pairing success in small tree finches: male and female body and beak size, male vocal performance, nest concealment, and male age.


Colouration

Small tree finches moult annually. Female small tree finches are grey/brown throughout their lives. Male ground finches are born grey/brown, similar to females, but through a pattern of annual moult, attain a black crown and chin (the upper part of their body). Age difference can be assessed in males by observing the darkness of their crown and chin.


Feeding
The small tree-finch is a mainly insectivorous bird, although has a varied diet. Typically, it gleans arthropods and leaves from bark surfaces, but 42% of its diet consists of vegetable matter (nectar, fruits, and seeds).

The '''small tree finch''' ('''''Camarhynchus parvulus''''') is a [[bird]] species belonging to the [[Darwin's finch]] group within the [[tanager]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Thraupidae]].
It has a grasping beak with curved culmens.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Tebbich|first1=Sabine|last2=Taborsky|first2=Michael|last3=Fessl|first3=Birgit|last4=Dvorak|first4=Michael|last5=Winkler|first5=Hans|date=2004-02-01|title=Feeding Behavior of Four Arboreal Darwin's Finches: Adaptations to Spatial and Seasonal Variability|url=https://academic.oup.com/condor/article/106/1/95/5563167|journal=The Condor|language=en|volume=106|issue=1|pages=95–105|doi=10.1093/condor/106.1.95|issn=0010-5422|doi-access=free|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical]] or tropical dry [[forest]]s and subtropical or tropical dry [[shrubland]]. During the non-breeding season it is known to form large groups with [[Small ground finch|small ground-finches]].

It is an endemic species to the Galapagos islands and its conservation status has been listed as "Least Concern".<ref name=iucn/><ref>{{Cite web|author=BirdLife International|title=Species factsheet: ''Geospiza parvula''|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/Small-Tree-Finch|access-date=1 March 2021|website=BirdLife International}}</ref> However, bird counts which have been conducted since 1997 have shown a continuous decline in small tree-finch populations in the ''Scalesia'' and agricultural zones of [[Santa Cruz Island (Galápagos)|Santa Cruz]], where the small tree-finch is most abundant. Small tree-finch counts have remained stable in the transition zone (also quite abundant here) and in the less popular dry and fern zones on Santa Cruz island.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Dvorak|first1=Michael|last2=Fessl|first2=Birgit|last3=Nemeth|first3=Erwin|last4=Kleindorfer|first4=Sonia|last5=Tebbich|first5=Sabine|date=January 2012|title=Distribution and abundance of Darwin's finches and other land birds on Santa Cruz Island, Galápagos: evidence for declining populations|journal=Oryx|language=en|volume=46|issue=1|pages=78–86|doi=10.1017/S0030605311000597|issn=0030-6053|doi-access=free}}</ref> This species is in particularly impacted by the larvae of the parasitic [[Philornis downsi|avian vampire fly]] (''Philornis downsi)''.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cimadom|first1=Arno|last2=Tebbich|first2=Sabine|date=January 2021|title=Timing of infestation influences virulence and parasite success in a dynamic multi-host–parasite interaction between the invasive parasite, Philornis downsi, and Darwin's finches|url= |journal=Oecologia|language=en|volume=195|issue=1|pages=249–259|doi=10.1007/s00442-020-04807-5|pmid=33258992|pmc=7882474|bibcode=2021Oecol.195..249C|issn=0029-8549}}</ref>

== Distribution ==
[[Image:Camarhynchus_parvulus.jpg|thumb|left|180px|A female bird]]
The small tree finch is found on all the main islands except Española, Genovesa, Marchena, Darwin, and Wolf. It was also absent in the most recent census (2017–2018) on Rábida, Pinta, and Santa Fé islands.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Kleindorfer|first1=Sonia|title=Field Guide: Resident landbirds of Galapagos|last2=Fessl|first2=Birgit|last3=Peters|first3=Katharina|last4=Anchundia|first4=David|publisher=Charles Darwin Foundation|year=2018|isbn=978-9978-53-063-4}}</ref>

== Mating ==
Darwin's tree finches build display domed nests and use these to attract mates and for nesting. Male small tree finches will sing close to their nest once it has been built, to attract female small tree finches. When these arrive, they inspect the display nest by entering it. Thereafter they will either choose to accept both the male and the display nest, accept the male but reject the nest (so they will then build a new nest together), or reject both the male and the display nest.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Kleindorfer|first=Sonia|date=October 2007|title=Nesting success in Darwin's small tree finch, Camarhynchus parvulus: evidence of female preference for older males and more concealed nests|url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0003347207002588|journal=Animal Behaviour|language=en|volume=74|issue=4|pages=795–804|doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2007.01.020|s2cid=53205559|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

Older males build more concealed nests, typically higher within the tree canopy, compared to younger males, which reduces predation on the nests. Females preferentially choose to breed with older males with whom they have higher fledging success compared to younger males. In a study by Kleindorfer (2007), predation accounted for 60% of nest loss, making it a strong selective force. Males with high vocal performance, which is predicted by beak morphology, also have higher pairing success. There is also evidence for size-assortative pairing based on beak and tarsus length. Thus multiple factors influence pairing success in small tree finches: male and female body and beak size, male vocal performance, nest concealment, and male age.<ref name=":1" />

== Colouration ==
[[File:Young male small tree finch at Los Gemelos on Santa Cruz Island.jpg|thumb|Young male small tree finch on [[Santa Cruz Island (Galápagos)|Santa Cruz Island]]]]
Small tree finches moult annually. Female small tree finches are grey/brown throughout their lives. Male ground finches are born grey/brown, similar to females, but through a pattern of annual moult, attain a black crown and chin (the upper part of their body). Age difference can be assessed in males by observing the darkness of their crown and chin.<ref name=":1" />

== Feeding ==
The small tree-finch is a mainly insectivorous bird, although has a varied diet. Typically, it gleans arthropods and leaves from bark surfaces, but 42% of its diet consists of vegetable matter (nectar, fruits, and seeds).<ref name=":0" />

zh.extract:
小树雀（学名：Camarhynchus parvulus），是雀形目唐纳雀科的一种鸟类。
小树雀体重约13.2克，翼长约61.3毫米，嘴峰长约11.2毫米，喙宽度约4.3毫米，喙厚度约6.5毫米，跗蹠长约19.3毫米，尾长约36.7毫米。小树雀在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的中等高度的林地中，食性为杂食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。


亚种
C. p. parvulus：分布于加拉帕戈斯群岛（查塔姆岛除外）的湿润灌丛。
C. p. salvini：分布于加拉帕戈斯群岛的圣克里斯托巴尔岛。
